summary.category: India's Drainage Systems and River Classification
points: India's drainage is classified into two major systems — Himalayan rivers (perennial with long courses, cutting through mountains creating gorges, performing intensive erosion in upper reaches and forming well-developed deltas) and Peninsular rivers (mostly seasonal with shorter courses, flowing primarily eastward into Bay of Bengal from Western Ghats water divide, except Narmada and Tapi which flow westward through rift valleys).

The three major Himalayan rivers are the Indus (2,900 km, originating in Tibet with tributaries in Kashmir), Ganga (2,500 km, formed by Bhagirathi and Alaknanda, joined by numerous tributaries including Yamuna and rivers from Nepal, forming world's largest Sundarban Delta), and Brahmaputra (flowing through Tibet, Arunachal Pradesh, and Assam with braided channels and the largest riverine island Majuli, causing frequent floods despite carrying massive water and silt loads).

Major Peninsular rivers include Godavari (largest peninsular river called Dakshin Ganga, 1,500 km), Mahanadi, Krishna, and Kaveri flowing eastward with smaller drainage basins; lakes in India form through glacial action (Himalayan lakes), river meandering (oxbow lakes), coastal deposition (Chilika, Pulicat), tectonic activity (Wular—largest freshwater), and human damming; rivers remain essential for irrigation, navigation, hydropower, and supporting civilization, though facing pollution threats addressed by Ganga Action Plan and National River Conservation Plan.
